title: Licensed Practical and Licensed Vocational Nurses
keyDrivers: Licensed practical nurses must be physically present to provide hands-on patient care, administering medications, changing dressings, and monitoring patients in unpredictable clinical settings that robots cannot navigate reliably. They carry real legal accountability through their nursing license and patients deeply value having a trusted human caregiver. AI will likely free them from charting and documentation burdens, letting them spend more time on direct care, while growing healthcare demand means more need for their skills, not less.

taskIntro: The highest-weight tasks in this role have no AI activity recorded - direct patient care, medication administration, vital sign measurement, and clinical observation are entirely absent from observed AI usage data. AEI coverage is only 9.1%, so patterns from the small amount of data available should not be generalized to the full role.

risks.body: AI is moving into documentation and charting work - the administrative layer of nursing that sits around direct patient care - which reduces time burden but also starts to overlap with some of what LPNs do between patient interactions [2]. Remote patient monitoring tools are automating some aspects of vital sign tracking and early deterioration alerts, which were traditionally LPN responsibilities in long-term care settings. The structural risk is not job elimination but role compression: as AI handles more paperwork and monitoring, the work that remains is increasingly the hands-on care that requires physical presence.

opportunities.body: This role scores a 4 out of 5 on physical presence requirements - medication administration, wound care, catheterization, and direct patient observation require someone in the room [2]. The physical care delivery tasks that define LPN work have no observed AI activity, and there is no credible near-term path for AI to perform them. BLS projects 6% employment growth through 2034, driven by aging population demand for long-term care and home health services where LPNs are the primary workforce.
opportunities.statLabel: projected employment growth through 2034 (BLS)

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The work most protected from AI is hands-on patient care - the physical tasks that require presence, touch, and real-time clinical judgment during a patient interaction. Build depth in patient assessment skills and complex care settings (long-term care, home health, rehabilitation) where LPN demand is growing fastest and AI tools are least embedded [2]. Over the next six months, get comfortable using AI-assisted documentation tools to reduce charting time - not because the skill is threatened, but because freeing time from paperwork means more patient-facing hours [2].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: LPN programs typically take 12 to 18 months and lead directly to licensure - it is one of the faster pathways into clinical healthcare [2]. The role is in highest demand in long-term care facilities, home health agencies, and physician offices, and the aging population creates structural tailwinds that are independent of AI trends. Clinical skills, not credential stacking, are what hiring managers want to see - prioritize programs with strong hands-on clinical hours [2].

careerCluster.title: Nurse Practitioner
careerCluster.fit: LPNs and LVNs already handle patient monitoring, medications, and direct care, but Nurse Practitioner is a clear step up that requires a graduate degree and gives you the authority to diagnose, create treatment plans, and prescribe independently.
steps: Earn RN license via bridge program (LPN-to-RN)
Complete MSN or DNP with NP specialization
Pass AANP or ANCC NP certification exam

careerCluster.title: Registered Nurse
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already handle vitals, medications, charting, and direct patient care, so the day-to-day work overlaps heavily, but RN is a step up — it requires a higher credential and gives you broader clinical authority, independent judgment, and supervisory responsibility over other nurses.
steps: Gain 1 year LPN clinical experience
Enroll in an LPN-to-RN bridge program
Pass the NCLEX-RN

careerCluster.title: Critical Care Nurse
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already monitor vitals, administer medications, and observe patient changes, but critical care nursing is a step up that requires an RN license and the ability to independently interpret complex data and manage life-threatening emergencies.
steps: Earn RN license via bridge LPN-to-RN program
Gain ICU or ER experience as new RN
Pass CCRN certification exam

careerCluster.title: Clinical Nurse Specialist
careerCluster.fit: LPN/LVN work gives you hands-on patient care and clinical observation skills that carry over, but the CNS role is a clear step up — it requires a master's degree, independent clinical judgment in a specialty, and responsibility for teaching other nurses and shaping care standards.
steps: Earn RN licensure via bridge program
Complete MSN with CNS specialty track
Obtain CNS certification from AACN or NACNS

careerCluster.title: Psychiatric Nurse
careerCluster.fit: Your hands-on patient care and medication administration experience carries over, but this is a significant step up that requires a master's or doctoral degree, RN licensure, and the authority to independently diagnose and prescribe.
steps: Earn RN license via ADN or BSN program
Complete MSN or DNP in psychiatric-mental health
Obtain PMHNP-BC certification from ANCC

careerCluster.title: Acute Care Nurse
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already handle vitals, medications, and patient monitoring, but Acute Care Nurses is a step up that requires an RN license and adds independent diagnosis, prescribing authority, and managing life-threatening conditions.
steps: Earn RN license via bridge LPN-to-RN program
Obtain ACNPC-AG or CCRN certification
Log clinical hours in ICU or emergency settings

careerCluster.title: Nurse Anesthetist
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already monitor vitals, administer medications, and observe patient responses, but Nurse Anesthetist is a significant step up requiring advanced clinical training, a master's or doctoral degree, and independent authority over anesthesia care.
steps: Earn a BSN, then RN license via NCLEX-RN
Gain 1-3 years ICU nursing experience
Complete accredited Nurse Anesthesia program (CRNA)

careerCluster.title: Nurse Midwife
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already handle patient monitoring, vitals, and basic care, but Nurse Midwife is a clear step up that requires an RN license first, then a graduate-level midwifery degree with specialized obstetric training.
steps: Earn RN license via bridge program (LPN-to-RN)
Complete accredited CNM graduate program (MSN or DNP)
Pass AMCB Certified Nurse-Midwife exam

careerCluster.title: Postsecondary Nursing Teacher
careerCluster.fit: Your hands-on clinical experience is real and directly useful in a teaching role, but this is a step up that requires a bachelor's or master's degree in nursing plus a shift from doing patient care to evaluating and instructing others who are learning to do it.
steps: Earn BSN or MSN from accredited nursing program
Apply for adjunct clinical instructor position
Obtain state nursing faculty certification if required

title: Air Traffic Controllers
jobTitles: Air Traffic Control Specialist/Certified Professional Controller (ATC Specialist/CPC)
keyDrivers: Air traffic controllers make life-or-death decisions in real time under ambiguous, rapidly changing conditions, the kind of high-stakes judgment where pattern-matching alone isn't enough. Regulations require a certified human to be responsible for separation and sequencing of aircraft, and society is unlikely to remove that requirement anytime soon. AI will likely serve as a powerful decision-support tool, making controllers more effective rather than replacing them, especially as air traffic volume grows.

taskIntro: None of this role's tasks appear in AI usage data. Air traffic control operates under FAA certification requirements and real-time safety protocols that restrict how AI tools can be used in operational settings - the absence from observed AI usage likely reflects regulatory constraints rather than technological inability.

risks.body: The long-term structural risk is not AI automation of the controller role but a shrinking pipeline of available positions. BLS projects employment to decline slightly as some ground coordination work is automated and operational efficiency improvements reduce staffing requirements at smaller facilities [2]. The career entry barrier is among the highest of any occupation: FAA Academy training is required, most hiring occurs before age 31, and the path runs almost exclusively through FAA or military experience.

opportunities.body: Air traffic control scores a 5 out of 5 on both novel real-time judgment and real-time response requirements - every traffic situation involves unique combinations of aircraft, weather, and runway conditions that require split-second decisions with no tolerance for error [2]. FAA regulations require human controllers to be the final authority on aircraft separation, and there is no regulatory pathway for AI to operate in this role without human oversight. Federal employment with strong union protections and a median salary above $137, 000 reflects the difficulty of replacing this work [2].
opportunities.statLabel: median annual salary (BLS 2024)

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The core skill - maintaining situational awareness across multiple simultaneous aircraft movements and making real-time separation decisions - is the most structurally protected work in this occupation and shows no AI footprint in usage data. Build proficiency with the NextGen automation tools that handle flight data and ground coordination, so that the human workload stays focused on the judgment layer rather than data processing [2]. Over the next several years, controller experience in high-traffic facilities or TRACON environments carries the most career leverage as smaller facilities see more automation of procedural work.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The path into air traffic control runs almost entirely through the FAA Academy at Oklahoma City or through military ATC training - there is no civilian community college or online certification route [2]. Most FAA hiring has an upper age cutoff around 31 and requires passing a biographical assessment and medical exam before Academy selection. The career is demanding but highly compensated and structurally protected from AI in the near term. Military ATC service is the most reliable alternative pathway for those who miss the FAA hiring window [2].

title: Computer Programmers
keyDrivers: Computer programmers who primarily write code to specifications are among the most directly threatened by AI, as code generation tools can already produce working software from natural-language descriptions. The role lacks physical requirements, legal protections, or deep relationship-based value, it's defined almost entirely by producing a functional output that AI is rapidly learning to deliver. While experienced programmers who architect solutions and understand complex systems will adapt, the traditional role of translating requirements into code is shrinking fast.

taskIntro: AI activity is spread broadly across this role's core work. The highest-weight tasks, writing and debugging code, all show measurable automation and augmentation signals, with thousands of real-world AI conversations recorded per task. This is one of the more thoroughly AI-contacted occupations in the data.

risks.body: The core of this job, writing and maintaining code, already shows 33% automation in real AI usage data across over 14,000 observed conversations. Revising and expanding existing programs runs even higher at 36% automation. GitHub's 2024 research found that developers using Copilot completed tasks 55% faster, which means fewer programmer-hours needed per project [1]. BLS projects computer programmer employment to decline 10% from 2023 to 2033, a loss of roughly 12,400 jobs, as companies consolidate programming work into broader software developer roles and offshore routine coding [2].
risks.statLabel: projected employment decline for computer programmers over 10 years

opportunities.body: The strongest augmentation signal sits in consulting with teams to resolve problems in running programs: 77% augmentation rate across 747 conversations. AI acts as a diagnostic partner here, but the human still drives the fix. The 2024 Stack Overflow Developer Survey found that 76% of developers are using or planning to use AI tools, but only 43% trust the accuracy of the output [3]. That trust gap is where experienced programmers add value: reviewing AI-generated code, catching subtle logic errors, and understanding system context that AI misses. Roles that combine programming skill with domain knowledge, like systems analysis or DevOps, are growing even as pure coding positions shrink.
opportunities.statLabel: augmentation rate for collaborative troubleshooting tasks

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI code-generation tools (GitHub Copilot, Claude, or similar) on real production tasks now, and track where they fail. The 2024 Stack Overflow survey found 43% of developers don't trust AI output accuracy [3], which means the ability to audit, debug, and correct AI-generated code is immediately valuable. Over the next six months, build depth in areas AI handles worst for this role: cross-team troubleshooting, understanding legacy system context, and translating business requirements into technical specs. Those collaborative, context-heavy tasks show the highest augmentation rates and lowest automation rates in the data.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Pure coding skills alone are a shrinking market. GitHub's research shows AI is already handling a meaningful share of routine code generation [1]. A stronger entry strategy: build a portfolio that demonstrates code review, debugging complex systems, and integrating components rather than writing greenfield code from scratch. Pair programming proficiency with a specific domain (healthcare data, financial systems, embedded hardware) to differentiate from both AI tools and generalist programmers. Consider aiming for software developer or DevOps roles rather than "computer programmer" titles specifically, since BLS projects software developers to grow 17% over the same period programmers decline [2].

quotes.quote: Developers with AI tools completed tasks 55% faster. The skill isn't prompting, it's evaluating what comes back and knowing when the output is subtly wrong.
76% of developers are using or planning to use AI tools in their development process, but only 43% trust the accuracy of AI output.

Employment of software developers is projected to grow 17 percent from 2023 to 2033, while computer programmers are projected to decline 10 percent, suggesting the market rewards broader engineering skills over pure coding.
Developers who pair coding with domain-specific knowledge and system design responsibilities report higher satisfaction and less concern about AI displacement.

careerCluster.title: Information Security Analyst
careerCluster.fit: Your programming background gives you a real head start — you already understand how software works, which makes spotting vulnerabilities easier — but this is a step up that requires learning threat monitoring, security protocols, and compliance frameworks you haven't been responsible for before.

Build a home lab, practice network monitoring
Study NIST cybersecurity framework basics

careerCluster.title: Computer Science Researcher
careerCluster.fit: You already write and debug complex programs, but this is a step up into original research, mathematical modeling, and designing the systems themselves rather than implementing specs others define.
steps: Earn a Master's or PhD in Computer Science
Publish or contribute to peer-reviewed research
Build projects applying novel algorithms or models

careerCluster.title: Software Developer

Study systems design (e.g., Grokking the System Design Interview)
Build 2-3 portfolio projects with documented architecture decisions

Build 2-3 portfolio projects with real datasets

Learn a backend framework like Node.js or Django

title: Software Developers
keyDrivers: Software developers are in a paradoxical position: AI code generation tools are already transforming the work, compressing routine coding tasks dramatically, yet developers who can architect systems, understand business needs, and manage AI-generated code will be more productive than ever. The role lacks physical presence or legal accountability protections, making pure coding work highly vulnerable. The strongest protection is that demand for software keeps growing, and experienced developers are well-positioned to direct and validate AI coding tools, but fewer developers may be needed to produce the same output.

Data manipulation and storage shows higher automation (42.6%) - AI handles more of the execution. Most top-weight tasks have no observed AI data.

risks.body: Data storage and retrieval work - the kind of database querying, data wrangling, and scripting that junior developers spend significant time on - shows 42.6% automation in over 2, 000 observed conversations, the clearest signal of AI handling real execution work in this role [1]. GitHub Copilot and similar tools are now used by 76% of developers, and entry-level hiring has contracted as AI handles more of the code-to-spec work that once required junior headcount [3].

opportunities.body: Analyzing user needs and software requirements - the highest-weight task with AI data - shows 44% augmentation across 4, 643 conversations, a strong signal that system design and requirements work keeps humans in the loop even as AI assists [1]. This role scores 4 out of 5 on both specialized expertise and directing AI tools, reflecting that the most valuable developer work involves architectural judgment and cross-system thinking that AI augments rather than replaces [2]. BLS projects 17% employment growth through 2034 - 40, 000+ annual openings - concentrated in roles requiring full-system ownership.

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The immediate move is to stop writing boilerplate code manually and start spending that time on the design decisions AI cannot make - system architecture, API contract design, cross-team requirements gathering. Over the next six months, build depth in the areas that show high augmentation rather than automation: requirements analysis, system design tradeoffs, and the stakeholder conversations that determine what gets built [1]. Developers who own the full system - requirements through deployment through operations - are more durable than those who specialize in execution [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The portfolio that gets hired in 2026 shows system-level thinking, not just clean code: deploy a full application with a database, API, and frontend, document the architectural decisions, and explain why you made them. Entry-level coding tasks are under the most direct AI pressure, so demonstrating judgment that goes beyond code output is how new developers differentiate [3]. BLS projects strong growth but the market has shifted - generic web development skills are less valuable than domain-specific technical depth or expertise in AI-assisted development workflows [2].

title: Information Security Analysts
jobTitles: Information Technology Security Analyst (IT Security Analyst)
keyDrivers: Security analysts face an ever-evolving landscape of threats where attackers constantly find novel ways to breach systems, making genuine human judgment in ambiguous, high-stakes situations essential. They spend enormous amounts of time on alert triage and log review that AI will compress, letting them focus on the harder investigative and strategic work. As AI expands the digital attack surface and creates new threat vectors, demand for skilled humans who can direct and validate AI-powered security tools will grow substantially.

taskIntro: The highest-weight tasks in this role have no AI activity recorded - core monitoring and safeguard planning work is absent from observed AI usage data. AI shows up further down the list in documentation and coordination work, where automation and augmentation signals are both present.

risks.body: Security policy documentation shows 41.2% automation in observed AI usage - the most direct signal that AI is handling a real slice of the written output work in this role [1]. The bigger structural risk is at the entry level: AI-assisted SIEM platforms and automated triage tools are absorbing tier-1 alert monitoring, which has historically been the on-ramp into the field [2].
risks.statLabel: automation rate on security documentation tasks

opportunities.body: Risk assessment and encryption work shows strong augmentation patterns - 47.9% and 58.7% respectively - with thousands of observed conversations where analysts stay firmly in the loop [1]. The role scores a 5 out of 5 on directing AI tools, and every security breach carries regulatory and legal consequences that organizations cannot delegate to automated systems [2]. BLS projects 28% employment growth through 2034, driven by demand for analysts who can interpret AI-flagged threats and respond to novel attack patterns.
opportunities.statLabel: projected employment growth through 2034 (BLS)

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start logging time against AI-assisted detection tools - specifically learning to configure and tune SIEM alert thresholds rather than just monitoring outputs. Over the next six months, build depth in incident response and threat hunting, the work that sits above automated triage and requires judgment under real operational pressure [2]. Analysts who can direct AI tools and own the investigation layer are pulling ahead of those whose value was in manual alert review [1].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The entry bar has shifted: Security+ certification plus hands-on lab work on TryHackMe or Hack The Box now signals more than a CS degree alone, because hiring managers want evidence of practical investigation skills, not just coursework [2]. Build a portfolio that shows you can operate in realistic attack scenarios - document a mock incident response from detection through remediation. The talent shortage is real and the growth projections are among the strongest in tech, but the jobs available increasingly require intermediate skills, not starter-level monitoring [2].

title: Computer and Information Systems Managers
keyDrivers: IT managers succeed because they deeply understand their organization's people, systems, and strategic priorities, context that can't be scraped from a database. Much of their value comes from navigating competing priorities across departments, managing vendor relationships, and making high-stakes technology decisions under uncertainty. As AI transforms every part of business, these managers become the key decision-makers directing how AI systems are adopted, integrated, and governed within their organizations.

taskIntro: The highest-weight tasks in this role have no AI activity recorded - directing operations, assigning work, and meeting with stakeholders are absent from observed AI usage data. The one task with AI signal is consulting with users on computing needs, where augmentation patterns dominate.

risks.body: As AI handles more of the technical work managed below this role - automated testing, code generation, infrastructure monitoring - IT department headcounts are shrinking, which can create pressure on management layers above them [2]. Consulting with users on computing needs shows 15.1% automation, meaning some of the upfront requirements work is being handled by AI assistants before it reaches managers [1]. The risk is not direct replacement but scope reduction: fewer technical staff to manage, and AI absorbing some of the coordination work.

opportunities.body: This role scores a 5 out of 5 on both interpersonal complexity and managing complex human systems - the decisions that move organizations forward involve trade-offs that require reading people and politics, not just processing data [2]. Consulting and vendor coordination work shows 53.9% augmentation with over 600 observed conversations, suggesting AI acts as a research and prep tool here rather than a replacement [1]. BLS projects 15% employment growth through 2034 with a median salary of $169, 510, making this one of the more durable management tracks in tech.
opportunities.statLabel: projected employment growth through 2034 (BLS)

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The decisions holding up best are the ones requiring organizational judgment - evaluating which AI tools to adopt, managing teams through workflow changes, and owning the security and reliability of systems that run on AI infrastructure. Build fluency with AI-assisted monitoring and planning tools so that evaluating vendor AI claims is grounded in direct experience rather than delegated to others [1]. Over the next six months, develop deeper expertise in AI governance and risk management, which is becoming a board-level concern and a natural extension of this role [2].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: This role typically requires 5 or more years in technical roles before the transition to management, and pathways through software development, security, or systems administration all lead here [2]. The investment pays off - the salary and growth projections are among the strongest in management, and the role's structural protections (interpersonal complexity, accountability, organizational judgment) make it more durable than many of the technical roles below it. Build a track record of owning cross-functional projects and making sound technology investment calls [2].

title: Web Developers
keyDrivers: Web development involves writing code for websites and applications, exactly the kind of well-structured, pattern-based work that AI coding tools are already handling with increasing competence. The role has no physical component, no legal accountability, and delivers output where clients care about results, not who or what built it. As AI code generation continues to improve, much of the standard web development workload can be completed faster and cheaper by AI-assisted workflows, significantly reducing demand for dedicated web developers.

risks.body: AI can already automate core web development tasks like building sites and writing supporting code, especially boilerplate and routine implementations. Entry-level tech hiring dropped 25% year-over-year in 2024, and software developer employment for workers aged 22-25 declined nearly 20% from its 2022 peak [1]. Developers who have not yet built depth in judgment, architecture, or system design are the most exposed.
risks.statLabel: drop in entry-level tech hiring, year-over-year (2024)

opportunities.body: 80% of developers now use AI tools, but trust in AI output collapsed from 40% to 29% year-over-year, and 66% say they spend more time fixing AI-generated code than they save writing it [2]. The emerging skill is knowing what to give AI, how to structure the problem, and how to catch if the output comes back wrong. Researchers are calling this context engineering [3].
opportunities.statLabel: of developers spend more time fixing AI code than they save writing it

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The immediate move is to treat AI output like untrusted code: review every diff, question every assumption, document the decisions AI does not make for you [4]. Over the next six months, build depth where AI handles things worst - system design, cross-team architecture decisions, and requirements too ambiguous for a model to interpret reliably [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Companies now expect AI literacy from day one - build a GitHub portfolio of real projects that shows you can direct AI tools, review their output, and ship working systems [5]. Code review discipline and catching AI failures matter more to hiring managers than volume of code written.

quotes.quote: Developers are ready to push back on enterprise AI through a nuanced conversation about trust, reliability, and the enduring value of human expertise.
Code review is a conversation, not a test.
We're entering an era where interns and junior developers are showing up already fluent in the best tools for AI code-gen on the market.
Being good isn't good enough.

emergingCareers.title: AI Integration Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Builds and maintains pipelines that connect large language models and AI APIs into existing web applications and backend systems. Designs prompt workflows, manages API orchestration, and ensures reliable AI feature delivery in production environments.

stat.text: Job postings requiring generative AI skills grew over 4x between 2022 and 2024, with integration-focused engineering roles among the fastest-growing subcategories.

emergingCareers.fit: Your existing skills in APIs, backend integration, and building web systems transfer directly, but you must shift from consuming APIs to orchestrating AI model behavior and managing unpredictable outputs in production.
steps: Complete DeepLearning.AI's LangChain short course on Udemy
Build a working chatbot using OpenAI API and LangChain in a personal project
Deploy a Hugging Face model via API into an existing web app you own

emergingCareers.title: Prompt Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Designs, tests, and optimizes prompts and instruction sets for LLM-powered products including chatbots, copilots, and content generation tools. Works closely with product and engineering teams to improve AI output quality, reliability, and safety.

stat.text: Prompt Engineer was listed among the top 25 fastest-growing job titles on LinkedIn in 2024, with a 76% year-over-year increase in postings.

emergingCareers.fit: Your experience building user-facing products and debugging systems transfers well, but you must shift from writing code that instructs machines to writing language that guides AI models.
steps: Complete DeepLearning.AI's free 'ChatGPT Prompt Engineering for Developers' course
Build a portfolio project using OpenAI Playground that solves a real content or chatbot problem
Learn Microsoft PromptFlow by following its official tutorial to test and evaluate prompt pipelines

emergingCareers.title: AI Product Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Bridges the gap between product management and engineering by building full-stack web features powered by AI models, owning everything from UI to model selection and evaluation. Takes AI prototypes from proof-of-concept to scalable, user-facing products.

stat.text: 76% of developers expect to use or are already using AI tools in their development process, with the majority integrating them directly into their coding workflow.

emergingCareers.fit: Your full-stack UI and API skills transfer directly, but you must shift from displaying data to designing around unpredictable model outputs and evaluation loops.
steps: Build a chatbot using Vercel AI SDK and OpenAI API with streaming responses
Complete Supabase's official full-stack Next.js tutorial, adding pgvector for semantic search
Ship a small public tool that lets users rate AI outputs, so you practice prompt iteration and evaluation

emergingCareers.title: Design Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Works at the intersection of Figma and production code, translating visual designs into polished, accessible React components and design systems. Owns the implementation layer between design and engineering: building component libraries, interaction patterns, and the pixel-level details that make products feel right.

stat.text: Figma's 2024 design-to-code report found that design-to-development handoff is the single biggest source of friction in product teams, with 60% of developers citing misaligned specs as a top time drain — the problem Design Engineers are hired to solve.
stat.sourceTitle: Bridging the gap between design and code

emergingCareers.fit: Web development's HTML, CSS, and React skills carry over directly, but the job shifts from building features to perfecting the craft of UI implementation — owning component fidelity, interaction quality, and design system coherence rather than feature delivery.
steps: Build and publish a React component library to npm with a Storybook docs site
Learn Framer Motion: rebuild one polished animation from a site you admire
Contribute to an open-source design system (shadcn/ui, Radix, MUI)

title: Web and Digital Interface Designers
keyDrivers: Web and digital interface design is one of the fields most directly disrupted by AI, tools can already generate layouts, prototypes, and even production-ready code from simple prompts. The work is fully remote, requires no licensing, and while human taste and user empathy still matter, clients are increasingly comfortable with AI-assisted or AI-generated design. Without strong legal protections or physical presence requirements, the typical practitioner in this role faces significant displacement pressure as AI design tools continue to improve rapidly.

risks.body: AEI task data shows that core deliverables for this role - building and maintaining websites and developing style guidelines - face automation rates of 41-45%, meaning a meaningful share of routine production work can now be generated without a designer in the loop [1]. Generative design tools are compressing the time required to produce initial wireframes, style sheets, and basic UI components, which tends to reduce the number of junior and mid-level seats needed per project. BLS projects only 9,100 annual openings against a field that has grown quickly, leaving the entry-level job market tighter than the headline +8% growth rate suggests [2].
risks.statLabel: website design tasks automatable by AI

opportunities.body: The strongest durability signal for this role is collaboration: tasks involving working with management on strategy and coordinating with development teams show augmentation rates above 36%, meaning AI assists rather than replaces the judgment calls that happen at the intersection of business goals and user needs [1]. Designers who own the brief - conducting user research, interpreting stakeholder feedback, and setting accessibility standards - are doing work that requires contextual reasoning and accountability that AI tools still handle poorly. The Stack Overflow 2024 Developer Survey found that 63% of developers cite 'AI tools lack context of codebase' as a key obstacle, a gap that a designer with deep product knowledge can fill [3].
opportunities.statLabel: AI augmentation rate for strategy collaboration tasks

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The immediate priority is shifting time toward the tasks AI cannot compress: user research sessions, accessibility audits, and cross-functional facilitation. Learning to use AI generation tools (Figma AI, Midjourney, v0) as a starting point rather than a finishing line - then critically editing and validating output against real user data - is the difference between being replaced by the tools and directing them. Over six months, building depth in design systems governance and component documentation creates a track record that is harder to replicate with prompts alone. Roles that bridge design and engineering, such as design technologist or product designer with front-end fluency, are holding up better in hiring than pure visual production roles.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: A portfolio that leads with the research and decision-making behind the designs carries more weight than one that showcases polished visuals alone, because hiring managers can now generate polished visuals cheaply. Concrete credentials worth pursuing include a Nielsen Norman Group UX Certificate, a Google UX Design Certificate (for foundational credibility), or a dedicated course in accessibility (WCAG 2.2 auditing, axe tools). Building in public - writing a case study that documents how user research shaped a specific design decision - demonstrates the kind of judgment that differentiates a mid-level candidate in a compressed market. Targeting industries with high regulatory or compliance requirements (healthcare, fintech, government) tends to insulate design roles from the fastest commoditization pressure.

title: Web Administrators
keyDrivers: Web administration involves managing servers, configurations, security patches, and uptime, tasks that are increasingly automated by cloud platforms and AI-driven infrastructure tools. The role requires no physical presence, no licensure, and limited novel judgment, leaving few natural defenses against automation. While organizational knowledge provides some buffer, the core tasks are well-defined and pattern-based, making this one of the more vulnerable technology roles.

risks.body: The highest-weight tasks in web administration are already showing significant AI involvement. Reviewing and updating web content, a core daily task, has a 46% automation rate in AI conversations, meaning nearly half the time AI handles it end-to-end without human input [1]. Troubleshooting server problems (weight 20.9) shows a lower 13% automation rate but a combined 38% AI involvement, and with AI coding assistants now used by 76% of developers, the tools that automate routine web maintenance are becoming standard issue [2]. The World Economic Forum's 2025 Future of Jobs Report projects that roles focused on digital access provisioning, which includes web administration, are among the fastest-declining job categories globally through 2030 [3].
risks.statLabel: automation rate for web content updates

opportunities.body: Security monitoring, the single most important task in this role by weight, shows a 27% augmentation rate alongside its 29% automation rate, meaning AI assists more often than it replaces the human on threat detection [1]. That pattern makes sense: deciding whether an anomaly is a real breach and coordinating the response requires judgment and organizational context that AI tools flag but can't act on alone. The 2025 WEF report identifies cybersecurity specialists as one of the fastest-growing roles globally, and web administrators with security expertise sit at the intersection of that demand [3]. Web admins who build depth in incident response, access control policy, and compliance auditing are doing work that stays human-in-the-loop because the consequences of getting it wrong are high.
opportunities.statLabel: augmentation rate on security monitoring tasks

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI-assisted monitoring and content management tools now, not to replace what you do, but to learn where they fail. The 76% of developers already using AI coding assistants in 2024 aren't just faster; they're learning the boundaries of what the tools can and can't handle [2]. Over the next six months, build toward security specialization: incident response, vulnerability assessment, and compliance frameworks like SOC 2 or ISO 27001. These are the areas where the data shows AI augments rather than automates, and where organizational accountability keeps a human in the decision seat [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: A pure web administration role is shrinking. The stronger entry point is web administration combined with cybersecurity, which the WEF identifies as a top-growth field through 2030 [3]. Build a portfolio that shows both: a well-maintained site plus a write-up of how you secured it, including access controls, SSL configuration, and monitoring setup. Certifications like CompTIA Security+ or AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner signal practical skill more than a general IT degree for this path.

keyDrivers: QA testing is heavily pattern-based work, writing test cases, running regression suites, and identifying bugs, that AI tools are already automating at increasing speed and accuracy. The role has no physical presence requirements, no licensing protections, and limited need for novel judgment, leaving few hard barriers against displacement. While some QA professionals will shift toward managing automated testing pipelines and evaluating AI-generated code, the overall number of positions is likely to shrink significantly as AI handles the bulk of testing work.

Providing feedback and recommendations to developers shows very high augmentation at 66.6% across over 4, 000 conversations - the clearest signal in this role that AI works as a thinking partner in quality feedback work rather than replacing it.

risks.body: AI code generation tools are writing tests automatically as part of development workflows, compressing the manual test writing work that has traditionally anchored this role [3]. Bug documentation - the highest-weight task with AI data - shows 21.8% automation across 1, 332 conversations, suggesting AI is handling some of the routine defect reporting and ticket creation [1]. This role has the lowest resilience score in the software career cluster, reflecting limited structural protections: most QA work lacks physical presence requirements, real-time judgment demands, or legal accountability that would insulate it from automation pressure.

opportunities.body: Providing feedback and recommendations to developers shows 66.6% augmentation across 4, 148 conversations - the strongest augmentation signal in this role, indicating AI acts as a research and analysis tool while humans own the quality judgment [1]. The work that holds up best is quality architecture: defining test strategies, evaluating system-wide risk, and communicating findings to engineering and product teams. Roles shifting toward quality engineering - writing test frameworks, building CI/CD pipelines, evaluating AI-generated code for correctness - are where QA skills are growing in value rather than declining [3].

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start moving from executing test cases to designing test strategy - the architectural decisions about what to test, how to structure automation frameworks, and how to evaluate AI-generated code are where human judgment matters most. Over the next six months, build skills in test automation engineering (Playwright, pytest, or Cypress) and CI/CD pipeline integration, since the QA roles growing in value are those that own the quality infrastructure rather than run scripts against it [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Manual QA testing as a standalone role is under meaningful pressure from AI code generation and automated testing tools, but quality engineering - the discipline of designing systems that catch bugs by construction - is growing in demand [3]. Build a portfolio that shows test framework design and automation engineering, not just test case execution. A background in software development alongside QA skills is increasingly the combination that leads to senior roles [2].

taskIntro: Nearly half of this role's tasks appear in AI usage data, with automation and augmentation running nearly even. Conducting logical analyses and building mathematical models - the one high-weight task with data - shows 38.6% augmentation and 24% automation across 3, 806 conversations, suggesting AI works as a collaborative tool in the core research work.

risks.body: Weighted automation across observed tasks runs at 38.9% - meaningful signal that AI is handling portions of the code writing, data analysis, and model evaluation work that researchers do alongside their primary investigations [1]. The risk is concentrated at the entry level: research assistants and junior scientists who spend the majority of their time on data processing and literature review face the most direct competition from AI tools that can handle those tasks. BLS projects 20% employment growth but notes that most opportunities will favor those with doctoral degrees and demonstrated research output [2].
risks.statLabel: weighted automation rate across observed tasks (AEI)

opportunities.body: This role scores a 5 out of 5 on novel judgment - the highest possible rating - reflecting that original scientific contribution, hypothesis generation, and working at knowledge frontiers are structurally resistant to automation [2]. Mathematical modeling and analysis shows 38.6% augmentation even as automation rises, indicating AI works as a research accelerator in the core work rather than displacing it entirely [1]. A10 scores a 5, meaning this role is well-positioned to direct and benefit from AI tools as they improve - researchers who use AI to run more experiments and test more hypotheses faster are producing more output with the same effort.
opportunities.statLabel: projected employment growth through 2034 (BLS)

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The research work most protected from displacement is the part that requires genuine originality - identifying novel problems, designing experiments that test real unknowns, and interpreting ambiguous results in context of the field. Start using AI tools to accelerate the literature review and initial modeling phases, which frees more time for the creative and judgment-intensive work at the frontier [1]. Over the next year, build depth in AI research itself or in domain-specific applications of machine learning, since those intersections carry both higher demand and stronger protections than generalist computer science research.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Doctoral degrees are the standard credential for this field, and most positions are in universities, government labs, and the research divisions of large technology companies [2]. The path is long but the role's structural protections are strong - original research output is among the hardest things for AI to generate reliably, and the judgment required to identify what is worth investigating is a persistent human advantage. Build a research portfolio that shows systems-level thinking and the ability to publish findings, not just implement existing methods [2].

taskIntro: None of this role's tasks appear in AI usage data. That absence is surprising given how directly data science work overlaps with AI tool capabilities - it likely reflects how O*NET categorizes data science tasks rather than a genuine lack of AI usage in the field.

risks.body: Exploratory data analysis, feature engineering, and initial model selection - work that junior data scientists and analysts spend significant time on - are tasks that AI tools now handle reliably and quickly [2]. AutoML platforms and AI-assisted analysis tools are automating the mechanical parts of the data science workflow, which concentrates demand at the senior end of the role where domain expertise and business judgment drive the work. BLS projects 34% employment growth, but hiring patterns suggest that demand is shifting toward data scientists who can translate complex model outputs into organizational decisions, not those who optimize model parameters.

opportunities.body: This role scores a 5 out of 5 on directing AI tools - the highest rating - reflecting that data scientists who use AI to run faster experiments, test more hypotheses, and explore larger datasets are producing more output than those doing the same work manually [2]. Domain expertise in healthcare, finance, climate science, or other specialized fields is what differentiates senior data scientists from AI tools and from each other. BLS projects 34% employment growth through 2034, among the strongest projections for any occupation, driven by organizations needing people who can interpret data model outputs and make consequential decisions based on them.
opportunities.statLabel: projected employment growth through 2034 (BLS)

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The parts of this role most resistant to displacement are problem formulation and decision translation - identifying which questions are worth asking with data, and communicating what model outputs actually mean for business or research decisions. Build depth in a specific domain (healthcare, finance, climate, operations) so that your value comes from understanding the context of the data, not just the mechanics of the analysis [2]. Over the next six months, get comfortable using AI tools to accelerate the EDA and preprocessing work, which frees more time for the judgment-intensive interpretation and stakeholder communication that AI does not handle well.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Most entry-level data scientist positions require at minimum a bachelor's degree in statistics, math, or computer science, and graduate degrees are common for research and senior roles [2]. The portfolio that gets hired shows end-to-end project work: data cleaning, modeling, and a clear explanation of what the findings mean for a real problem. Domain knowledge matters more as AI tools commoditize general-purpose data analysis - specialize in a field where you understand the data's context, not just its structure [2].

taskIntro: The highest-weight tasks in this role have no AI activity recorded - stakeholder coordination, meeting facilitation, project execution oversight, and milestone tracking are absent from observed AI usage data. AI shows up in about 29% of tasks by count, mostly in documentation and planning support work.

risks.body: Weighted automation on the observed tasks runs at 34.7%, with AI tools like Notion AI, Jira automation, and AI-assisted reporting handling status updates, meeting notes, and routine project documentation [1]. These tasks are real parts of a project manager's week even if they sit below the highest-weight coordination work, and their automation compresses the hours spent on admin. The structural risk is not displacement of the role but commoditization of the parts that were historically junior or coordinator-level entry points [2].
risks.statLabel: weighted automation rate across observed tasks (AEI)

opportunities.body: This role scores a 4 out of 5 on both human relations complexity and managing complex human systems - the work of aligning stakeholders, managing competing priorities, and making judgment calls when projects go sideways is structurally hard to automate [2]. The core value of IT project management sits in the judgment layer: knowing when to escalate, how to negotiate scope, and which technical risks matter to business outcomes - none of which appears in observed AI usage data. As AI-assisted development teams move faster, demand is growing for project managers who can coordinate across AI-augmented workflows and manage the faster delivery cycles that result [3].
opportunities.statLabel: projected employment growth through 2034 (BLS)

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Adopt AI-assisted project management tools (Notion AI, Linear, GitHub Projects with automation) to eliminate the documentation and status-reporting overhead, which frees more time for the stakeholder work that AI cannot do. Over the next six months, build depth in managing AI-assisted development teams specifically - understanding the delivery dynamics when developers are using Copilot and agents changes how you estimate, scope, and track progress [3]. PMs who can credibly evaluate AI tooling decisions and translate technical AI risk into business terms are increasingly valuable at the executive level.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Most IT project manager roles require a combination of technical background and demonstrated delivery experience - PMP or CAPM certification signals process discipline, but hiring managers want to see projects actually shipped [2]. The fastest path is through technical roles first: software development, QA, or systems administration all provide the foundational credibility that makes IT PM work legible. Agile and Scrum certifications are table stakes in most software organizations [3].

title: Retail Salespersons
keyDrivers: Retail sales is increasingly moving online, and even in-store, self-checkout and AI-powered recommendation tools are reducing the need for human salespeople. The work involves limited judgment, no legal accountability, and customers often prefer browsing independently. Physical presence on the sales floor offers some protection, but it's not enough to offset the steady erosion from e-commerce and automation.

taskIntro: AI activity in retail sales clusters around product recommendation and customer Q&A tasks, which sit in the middle of the weight distribution. The highest-weight task, greeting customers and identifying needs, shows no automation or augmentation signal despite over a thousand AI conversations recorded. Physical tasks like packaging purchases and operating cash registers have no AI activity recorded at all.

risks.body: The product recommendation task, one of the heaviest in the role, already shows a 43% automation rate in AI usage data. AI-powered recommendation engines and conversational shopping assistants are handling a meaningful share of the "help the customer find what they want" workflow, which is the core of retail selling. Salesforce's 2024 State of Sales report found that 81% of sales teams are either using or experimenting with generative AI, with customer-facing product guidance among the top use cases [1]. Meanwhile, BLS projects retail salesperson employment to decline 2% over the next decade, a loss of roughly 86,000 positions, as self-checkout, e-commerce, and AI chat tools reduce headcount needs [2].
risks.statLabel: automation rate on product recommendation tasks

opportunities.body: Describing merchandise and explaining product use, operation, and care shows a 43% augmentation rate, the highest in the role. This is the consultative, hands-on side of retail selling: demonstrating a product, troubleshooting fit, explaining compatibility. AI can surface specs and comparisons, but the in-person explanation still relies on a human. HubSpot's 2024 State of Sales report found that 78% of salespeople using AI tools said AI helped them spend more time on actual selling by offloading research and admin work [3]. Answering store and merchandise questions also shows a 29% augmentation rate, suggesting AI is supplementing product knowledge rather than replacing the interaction.
opportunities.statLabel: of salespeople say AI frees time for selling

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI product-lookup tools (chatbots, internal knowledge bases) to answer routine inventory and spec questions faster, so more floor time goes to hands-on demos and consultative conversations. Over the next six months, build depth in the areas AI handles worst in this role: live product demonstration, fitting and compatibility troubleshooting, and post-sale relationship building. HubSpot's data shows salespeople who pair AI research tools with face-to-face selling report higher close rates than those relying on either alone [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The retail sales jobs with the longest shelf life are the ones that require real product expertise and in-person interaction: specialty retail (electronics, outdoor gear, automotive), showroom sales, and high-touch customer service roles. Generic cashier-plus-greeter positions are the most exposed to self-checkout and AI chat. Salesforce's research found that sales roles emphasizing relationship management and complex product knowledge were the least likely to see AI-driven headcount reductions [1]. If entering retail, target roles where explaining, demonstrating, or customizing the product is the main job, not just ringing it up.

quotes.quote: Sellers who use AI to handle research and data entry are spending 12% more time actually selling, and reporting higher quota attainment as a result.
The most effective sales teams are using AI for customer intelligence and then doubling their investment in face-to-face relationship building, not choosing one over the other.
Sales roles that combine product expertise with consultative interaction are seeing the strongest demand growth, even as transactional roles flatten.
Salespeople who develop skills in AI-assisted customer research alongside traditional product knowledge report faster ramp-up times and stronger early performance.

title: Insurance Sales Agents
keyDrivers: People buy insurance from someone they trust, especially for complex policies involving life, business, or major assets. The human relationship and ability to understand a client's unique situation provide real protection against automation. However, simpler insurance products are increasingly sold online without agents, and AI tools can now handle routine quoting and policy comparisons, which puts pressure on the lower end of this profession.

taskIntro: AI activity in this role clusters around client-facing advisory and documentation tasks, where both automation and augmentation signals appear. The highest-weight sales and client acquisition tasks have no AI activity recorded in the available data.

risks.body: Direct-to-consumer insurance platforms like Lemonade, Policygenius, and embedded insurance at point of sale are compressing the role of the traditional agent in personal lines. McKinsey estimates that personal-lines distribution could see agent headcount decline as carriers shift to digital-first channels [1]. BLS projects just 6% growth for insurance sales agents through 2033, slower than the average for all occupations [2]. The biggest pressure point is simple, standardized products like auto and renters insurance, where comparison tools and AI-driven quoting engines let customers skip the agent entirely.
risks.statLabel: projected job growth through 2033, below the national average

opportunities.body: Complex commercial lines, life insurance, and high-net-worth personal coverage still depend heavily on relationship-driven advising that AI tools struggle to replicate. Salesforce's 2024 State of Sales report found that 83% of sales representatives using AI tools reported revenue growth in the prior year, suggesting agents who adopt AI for prospecting and quoting can sell more, not less [3]. LIMRA research shows that the "advice gap" in life insurance remains wide: roughly 102 million U.S. adults say they need more life insurance coverage than they have, a demand pool that favors human advisors who can navigate underwriting complexity and emotional buying decisions [4].
opportunities.statLabel: U.S. adults say they need more life insurance

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI-powered quoting and lead-scoring tools now. Platforms like Salesforce Einstein and carrier-specific CRM integrations can automate routine quote comparisons and flag high-intent prospects, freeing up time for consultative selling [3]. Over the next six months, build deeper expertise in complex product lines (commercial, life, umbrella) where the advisory relationship matters most. These are the areas where AI handles the least: understanding a client's full risk picture, navigating multi-carrier underwriting, and guiding emotionally loaded decisions like life insurance.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The strongest entry path is through commercial lines or life insurance, not personal auto or renters, where digital platforms are already dominant. Earning a state license is the baseline, but stacking a Chartered Property Casualty Underwriter (CPCU) or Certified Insurance Counselor (CIC) designation signals specialization that sets candidates apart. LIMRA's research on the advice gap shows that demand for human advisors is concentrated among clients with complex needs, so building a book in that space from the start is a better long-term bet than volume-based personal lines [4].

quotes.quote: Sales reps who use AI tools for lead prioritization and administrative tasks are 1.3× more likely to have seen revenue increase, the advantage comes from reinvesting that time in complex client conversations.

As insurers accelerate digital-direct channels for standard products, agents who reposition toward advisory roles in commercial and specialty lines capture higher commissions and stickier client relationships.
There are 102 million American adults who say they don't have enough life insurance, the need for knowledgeable advisors to close that gap isn't shrinking, it's growing.

The shift to digital quoting in personal lines means new agents should build their initial book around commercial accounts and complex personal coverage, where the advisory relationship is the product.

taskIntro: AI activity in this role splits along a clear line. The customer-facing tasks that carry the most weight, like answering product questions and making recommendations, show meaningful AI involvement with both automation and augmentation patterns. The relationship-heavy and in-person tasks, such as negotiating contracts, stocking displays, and providing samples, have no recorded AI activity.

risks.body: The two highest-weight tasks in this role, answering customer questions and recommending products, already show automation rates of 37% and 33% respectively. That's significant because those tasks are the core of inside and routine sales work. AI-powered product configurators, chatbots, and CRM-driven recommendation engines are handling a growing share of what used to require a rep on the phone or in an email thread. Salesforce's 2024 State of Sales report found that 81% of sales teams are either using or experimenting with generative AI, with lead qualification and customer communications among the top use cases [1]. The broader pattern: routine order-taking and informational sales calls are increasingly self-service, which compresses demand for reps whose value is primarily transactional.
risks.statLabel: of sales teams using or piloting generative AI

opportunities.body: The augmentation signal on answering customer questions is 42%, the highest of any task with sufficient data in this role. That means AI is frequently used as a co-pilot rather than a replacement, helping reps pull up specs, pricing history, and competitive comparisons in real time. HubSpot's 2024 State of Sales report found that sales professionals using AI tools save roughly two hours per day on manual tasks like data entry and note-taking, freeing time for relationship-building and complex deal negotiation [2]. The tasks with no AI signal, negotiating contracts, managing retail merchandising relationships, and in-person client work, are structurally harder to automate because they depend on reading a room, building trust over time, and handling ambiguity. Reps who spend most of their time on consultative and relationship-driven selling are in a fundamentally different position than those doing transactional order fulfillment.
opportunities.statLabel: augmentation rate on customer Q&A tasks

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI tools for pre-call research and proposal drafting now. CRM platforms like Salesforce and HubSpot already embed generative AI for call summaries, email drafts, and deal scoring, and reps who use them report saving roughly two hours a day on administrative work [2]. Over the next six months, focus on deepening consultative selling skills: complex multi-stakeholder negotiations, custom solution design, and in-person relationship management. These are the tasks where AI has no recorded activity and where the structural barriers to automation, reading body language, navigating politics, building trust, are highest.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The version of this role worth entering is consultative B2B sales, not transactional order-taking. Look for positions where the sales cycle is long, the deal size is large, and the product requires customization or technical explanation. When building a resume, demonstrate competency with CRM and AI-assisted sales tools like Salesforce Einstein or HubSpot's AI features. LinkedIn's 2024 State of Sales report found that 65% of B2B buyers say a salesperson who demonstrates deep understanding of their business is the most important factor in winning a deal [3]. That's the skill that matters, and it's hard to fake on a resume without real examples of complex deals closed.

quotes.quote: Generative AI is helping sellers spend more time actually selling, top performers use AI for research, outreach, and CRM updates so they can focus on conversations that close deals.
Sales professionals who use AI to automate manual tasks like data entry and note-taking save an average of two hours and 15 minutes per day.
65% of B2B buyers say a rep who understands their specific business needs and pain points is more important than pricing in their purchase decision.
The highest-performing sales teams are 1.9 times more likely to use AI, but they pair it with deeper product expertise and account strategy, not just faster outreach.

title: Real Estate Sales Agents
keyDrivers: Sales agents show properties, build client relationships, and handle licensed transaction work, but much of their day-to-day involves tasks AI is rapidly improving at, lead generation, market comparisons, scheduling, and initial client matching. While licensing requirements and the importance of in-person showings provide some protection, agents face more displacement risk than brokers because they typically handle more routine transactions with less complex judgment. The large number of agents relative to market need makes this role vulnerable to consolidation as AI tools make each agent more productive.

taskIntro: AI activity is spread across most of this role's highest-weight tasks, with automation signals strongest in property matching, marketing, and client Q&A. The negotiation and in-person coordination tasks show lower automation rates or too few observations to draw conclusions.

risks.body: The tasks AI hits hardest are the ones agents spend the most time on. Property matching (generating lists for buyers) shows 49% automation, marketing and listing promotion shows 41%, and answering client questions about financing and repairs shows 42%. These are core daily activities, not back-office busywork. NAR's 2024 member profile found that 35% of Realtors already use AI tools in their business, up from near zero two years prior [1]. Zillow and similar platforms are increasingly offering AI-powered home valuations and instant property recommendations directly to consumers, compressing the information advantage that agents have traditionally held [2].

opportunities.body: Negotiation, the task where agents act as intermediaries between buyers and sellers, shows a 29% automation rate but sits at a weight of 21.8. That's the lowest automation rate among the high-weight tasks with enough data, and the task itself depends on reading people, managing emotions, and navigating one-off deal structures that AI handles poorly. NAR data shows 88% of buyers purchased their home through an agent in 2024, and the top reason cited was help negotiating terms [1]. The augmentation signal on client Q&A (22%) suggests AI is useful as a research assistant for agents fielding questions about financing and appraisals, but clients still want a human explaining what it means for their specific situation. Transaction complexity also works in agents' favor: coordinating escrow, inspections, lenders, and pest control across a closing timeline involves real-time judgment calls that resist automation.
opportunities.statLabel: of buyers used an agent to purchase a home

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI tools for the property-matching and CMA (comparative market analysis) work that already shows high automation rates. Agents who let AI handle the initial search filtering and pricing comps can spend that time on the parts AI handles worst: face-to-face negotiation, reading client anxiety during walkthroughs, and managing the multi-party coordination of a closing. NAR found that agents using AI tools reported spending more time on client relationships and less on administrative tasks [1]. Over six months, building deeper expertise in complex transactions like multi-offer situations, 1031 exchanges, or investment property analysis creates a niche that automated platforms can't replicate.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The barrier to entry in real estate is low (a state license, typically a few weeks of coursework), but that's also the problem. Low barriers mean lots of competition, and AI is making the commodity part of the job (finding listings, pulling comps) nearly free. New agents who build a reputation around a specific transaction type or neighborhood tend to survive the first two years at much higher rates. Zillow's research shows that hyperlocal market knowledge remains one of the top reasons sellers choose a specific agent [2]. Focus early on learning the negotiation and closing coordination side, not the listing side that platforms are absorbing.

quotes.quote: Members who have integrated AI into their workflow report that they can better focus on building relationships, negotiating on behalf of their clients, and providing the personalized guidance that defines great service.
The agents who differentiate themselves are those who understand the complexities of a transaction, managing inspections, appraisals, and lender timelines, not just the listing process.

Consumers increasingly start their search online, but they still turn to agents when the transaction gets complicated, negotiation, contingency management, and closing coordination are where agent value is most visible.
The real estate landscape is shifting toward a model where technology handles discovery and agents handle trust, local expertise, pricing judgment, and advocacy through the close.

The role is fundamentally built on trust, as people making the biggest financial decision of their lives want a human advocate who understands their needs and can negotiate on their behalf. risks.body: Online platforms like Zillow, Redfin, and Opendoor have steadily absorbed functions that brokers once controlled exclusively, from property search to automated valuations to iBuying. NAR data shows total Realtor membership declined from a peak of roughly 1.56 million in 2022 to about 1.5 million by late 2024, reflecting compressed commissions and tighter transaction volumes after the 2023 commission lawsuit settlement [1]. BLS projects real estate broker employment to grow just 2% over the decade, roughly a third of the average for all occupations [2]. The combination of platform displacement and a legal shift toward negotiable commissions is squeezing the middle of the market hardest.

opportunities.body: NAR's 2024 buyer survey found that 88% of recent buyers purchased through an agent or broker, a figure that has barely moved in a decade despite the rise of online search tools [1]. Complex transactions, multi-property portfolios, and commercial deals still demand someone who can negotiate, interpret local regulations, and manage counterparty relationships. Brokers who use AI-powered CMA (comparative market analysis) tools and predictive analytics for lead scoring report closing deals faster, and Zillow Research notes that AI-enhanced listing descriptions generate 15% more saves on average compared to standard listings [3]. The brokerage model is shifting from gatekeeping information to providing judgment, local expertise, and transaction management that platforms cannot replicate.

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI-powered CMA and lead-scoring tools now. Platforms like HouseCanary and Zillow's Zestimate API can generate comps in seconds, but interpreting those comps for a specific deal still requires local knowledge and negotiation context [3]. Over the next six months, build depth in the parts of brokerage AI handles worst: complex commercial transactions, 1031 exchanges, multi-party negotiations, and local zoning interpretation. These tasks involve too many variables and too much interpersonal judgment for current AI to handle reliably.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The post-settlement commission landscape means new brokers need to demonstrate clear value from day one. Focus on a geographic niche or property type (luxury, multifamily, land) where deep local knowledge is a genuine differentiator rather than competing on volume in a commoditized residential market [1]. Getting a broker's license is table stakes. What separates successful entrants now is fluency with transaction technology, CRM systems, and data-driven pricing, combined with a network built through actual deal experience, not just coursework.

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start by getting fluent in the AI tools the sales team is already using or about to adopt. CRM-embedded AI features like Salesforce Einstein or HubSpot's predictive lead scoring are the fastest way to see what the data actually shows versus what reps report anecdotally [2]. Over six months, build depth in the areas AI handles worst: coaching underperformers through slumps, negotiating custom enterprise deals, and reading the political dynamics inside a client's buying committee. These tasks barely register in AI usage data because they require real-time social judgment that no model replicates well.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The path into sales management almost always runs through a few years as an individual contributor first. When building that track record, focus on complex or consultative sales (enterprise software, medical devices, industrial equipment) rather than transactional sales, since transactional pipelines are the first to get AI-automated [2]. Demonstrating that you can run a forecast, diagnose why a deal stalled, and coach a junior rep through it is more valuable than any single certification. Familiarity with CRM analytics and AI-driven pipeline tools is table stakes for new managers now, so get hands-on with them as a rep, not after promotion [3].

quotes.quote: Sales leaders are now expected to interpret AI-generated insights, not just review dashboards, and translate them into coaching moments for their teams.
The top-performing salespeople don't just use AI to save time, they use the time saved to deepen relationships and handle more complex deals.
Reps who actively use AI tools for prospecting and pipeline management are promoted to management roles faster, because they already think in terms of data-driven strategy rather than gut instinct.
As AI automates routine pipeline tasks, the premium shifts to managers who can handle ambiguity, reading a room, structuring a creative deal, motivating a demoralized team.
